Capital gains tax calculator

Estimate your capital gains tax, effective tax rate, and after-tax proceeds in seconds for stocks, real estate, and other investments.

Optimized for current tax rules. For guidance only — always confirm with a qualified tax professional in your country.

No converter config found for this tool.

Pro outputs

    How the capital gains tax calculator works

    This calculator helps you estimate how much tax you may owe when you sell an investment for more than you paid for it. It focuses on three key outputs: your capital gain or loss, the estimated tax due, and your after-tax proceeds.

    You typically enter the purchase price, selling price, and any allowable costs, such as broker commissions or improvement costs for real estate. The tool then applies the tax rate that matches your holding period or tax bracket to estimate the tax due.

    Basic capital gains formula

    At its core, capital gains tax is based on your taxable gain. The following plain-text formulas show the typical steps. 

    capital gain = selling price - purchase price - allowable costs
tax due = capital gain * tax rate
after tax proceeds = selling price - tax due

    If the result of the first line is negative, you have a capital loss instead of a gain. Many tax systems allow you to offset gains with losses, up to certain limits.

    Short term vs long term gains

    Many tax systems distinguish between short term and long term capital gains. Short term gains apply when you hold an asset for a relatively short period, such as less than one year. These gains are often taxed at your regular income tax rate.

    Long term gains apply when you hold an asset beyond a minimum holding period, such as more than one year. Long term gains often benefit from reduced tax rates or special allowances. The calculator can help you compare the impact of different holding periods by adjusting the tax rate input.

    What counts as allowable costs

    Allowable costs reduce your taxable gain. Common examples include:

    • Brokerage commissions when buying or selling investments
    • Legal and notary fees related to the purchase or sale
    • Documented improvement costs for real estate, such as structural renovations
    • Transaction taxes or stamp duties that can be added to cost basis

    Rules differ by country, so always check which costs are recognized by your local tax authority.

    Real world example

    Imagine you bought shares in a company for 10,000 currency units and paid 50 in broker fees. Two years later, you sell the shares for 16,000 and pay another 50 in broker fees. Your total purchase cost is 10,050 and your net selling amount is 15,950. 

    capital gain = 15,950 - 10,050 = 5,900

    If your long term capital gains tax rate is 15 percent, the estimated tax due is: 

    tax due = 5,900 * 0.15 = 885
after tax proceeds = 15,950 - 885 = 15,065

    By adjusting the inputs in the calculator, you can quickly see how different purchase prices, selling prices, or tax rates change your after-tax proceeds.

    Capital gains tax FAQs

    How is capital gains tax calculated?
    Capital gains tax is usually calculated as tax rate multiplied by your taxable gain. The taxable gain is the selling price minus the purchase price and allowable costs such as broker fees or improvement costs. Some tax systems apply different rates for short term and long term gains, and may also include exemptions or tax-free allowances.
    What is the difference between short term and long term capital gains?
    Short term capital gains apply when you hold an asset for a relatively short period, for example less than one year. These gains are often taxed at your regular income tax rate. Long term capital gains apply when you hold an asset beyond a defined minimum holding period, such as more than one year, and they often benefit from reduced tax rates or special allowances.
    Which assets are subject to capital gains tax?
    Capital gains tax can apply to many types of assets, including stocks, bonds, mutual funds, real estate, business assets, and some collectibles. Many tax systems provide exemptions for primary residences, small gains below a threshold, or specific retirement accounts. Always check the rules that apply in your country or region.

    Disclaimer

    This capital gains tax calculator is for informational and educational purposes only. It simplifies many aspects of tax law and does not cover every possible exemption, allowance, or special rule. The results are estimates and do not constitute tax, legal, or financial advice. Always review your situation with a qualified professional or your local tax authority before making decisions.
